sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=14).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board is split left/right by a white-block divider. Left side (charcoal bg) has colored objects: (1) gray box with yellow center + blue square, (2) gray box + red bar + dark red box, (3) scattered blue/charcoal checkerboard pattern, (4) gray box with light green center. Right side (black bg) has two white outline containers: one with red interior, one with blue interior. The scattered pattern at rows 34-37 might be a cursor/selection indicator.
- Goal model: Likely a matching puzzle - need to match left-side objects to right-side containers by color.
- Plan: Test interaction by clicking on the red bar (left side) to see if it transfers to the red container on the right.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after clicking on the red bar, but the nodes look the same. Let me compare before and after more carefully to see what actually changed. The `board_changed` was True, so something did change. Let me compare the segmentation between the previous frame and current frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the mouse click
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

# Build dicts by id for comparison
before_dict = {n['id']: n for n in before}
after_dict = {n['id']: n for n in after}

print("Changes detected:")
all_ids = set(list(before_dict.keys()) + list(after_dict.keys()))
for nid in sorted(all_ids):
    b = before_dict.get(nid)
    a = after_dict.get(nid)
    if b is None:
        print(f"  NEW: id={nid}, color={a['color']}, pixels={a['pixels']}")
    elif a is None:
        print(f"  REMOVED: id={nid}, color={b['color']}, pixels={b['pixels']}")
    elif b['boundary'] != a['boundary']:
        print(f"  MOVED: id={nid}, color={a['color']}, {b['boundary']} -> {a['boundary']}")
    elif b['pixels'] != a['pixels']:
        print(f"  SIZE CHANGED: id={nid}, color={a['color']}, {b['pixels']} -> {a['pixels']}")
    elif b['color'] != a['color']:
        print(f"  COLOR CHANGED: id={nid}, {b['color']} -> {a['color']}")

# Check adjacency changes
before_adj = set(tuple(sorted(x)) for x in last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['adjacency_list'])
after_adj = set(tuple(sorted(x)) for x in current_frame.segmentation['adjacency_list'])
if before_adj != after_adj:
    print(f"\nAdjacency changes: {len(before_adj - after_adj)} removed, {len(after_adj - before_adj)} added")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The only changes were to the bottom border area - the G (dark gray) and W (white) regions at the bottom. These are likely HUD/timer elements, not gameplay objects. The actual game objects didn't change at all.
So clicking on the red bar didn't do anything meaningful to the game state. Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to use arrow keys to move something, or maybe I need to click on different objects.
Let me try clicking on the scattered blue/charcoal pattern area, since that might be a cursor or interactive element. Or maybe I need to try arrow keys.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the red bar only changed the bottom border (likely a timer/HUD), not gameplay objects. The game objects remained unchanged. Need to try different interactions - perhaps arrow keys move a cursor, or the scattered pattern is the interactive element.
Plan: Try arrow key movements to see if something moves on the board, or try clicking on the scattered blue/charcoal checkerboard pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try pressing RIGHT to see if something moves
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print("Action result:", last_action_result)

# Compare before and after
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
before_dict = {n['id']: n for n in before}
after_dict = {n['id']: n for n in after}

print("\nChanges:")
all_ids = set(list(before_dict.keys()) + list(after_dict.keys()))
for nid in sorted(all_ids):
    b = before_dict.get(nid)
    a = after_dict.get(nid)
    if b is None or a is None or b['boundary'] != a['boundary'] or b['pixels'] != a['pixels'] or b['color'] != a['color']:
        if b and a:
            if b['boundary'] != a['boundary']:
                print(f"  MOVED: id={nid}, color={a['color']}, pixels={a['pixels']}")
                print(f"    {b['boundary']} -> {a['boundary']}")
            elif b['pixels'] != a['pixels']:
                print(f"  SIZE: id={nid}, {b['pixels']} -> {a['pixels']}")
            elif b['color'] != a['color']:
                print(f"  COLOR: id={nid}, {b['color']} -> {a['color']}")
        elif b:
            print(f"  REMOVED: id={nid}, color={b['color']}")
        else:
            print(f"  NEW: id={nid}, color={a['color']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
